transcript.transcript: And we used to, um, some of the, some of the, uh, big houses there had, uh, apple, uh, apple trees. And we would come up from the river, walk from the river and pick apples, um, uh, from, uh, from some of the properties, the, um, at the and the only other thing, Fiona, that I, uh, can remember is that I would say to myself, one of these days I'm going to own one of these properties. And, uh, sure enough, uh, in 1982, I bought one of the mansions on Woodside Circle, uh, where, um, uh, the Park River ran behind. Um, and, uh, I don't know whether you know this, but at one point it the Park River, it was lovingly called the hog River in those days, informally, um, ran all the way down to near, near Bushnell Park, under under Farmington Avenue and, and, uh, all the way down near to Bushnell Park. Right. In 1955, um, there was a, uh, there was a major flood, uh, and, um, the the river rose and, uh, we had to evacuate our, our our, uh, our apartment. Um, did everyone in Westbrook have to evacuate? Well, at least those who were on Mark Twain Drive did. Okay. Which is where we were, uh. and we ended up spending, uh, I would, I would say a good part of the summer, uh, uh, at my aunt's, uh, apartment in Stowe Village, which was also a relatively new public housing, um, development, um, in North Hartford. Did would you say that the, um, the folks who are moving into Westbrooke Village and Stowe Village, um, like, truly felt like this was a turning point for Hartford's housing access and and availability. Yeah, I would think so. Yes yes, yes I would. Yes, I certainly would think that that would be the case. And mostly because, you know, they were they were um, they were new. Um, they were new housing developments. Um, Westbrook village, Bowles Park, Stowe Village. Um W square was much older at that time. Um, and, you know, a lot of people were moving, um, from, uh, that part of North Hartford, Bellevue, Bellevue Street, Sanford Street, Worcester Street. Um, you know, my grandparents and several of my aunts and uncles lived on Worcester Street right next to Faith Congregational Church for many, many years. And they ended up moving, uh, to Westbrook Village. Um, so that was a turning point, primarily because these were brand new developments that took, uh, that were developed in the early 1950s. What do you have any sense of? Um, so your family members who lived over in the Bellevue side, do you have any sense of how welcoming they were of of the Bellevue Square? Um, Bellevue Square project. I've done some interviews of folks who talk about the the businesses that went away and didn't come back, or the houses that were razed to make way for Bellevue Square. So there's been sort of a mixed. I've had some mixed pictures, depending on who's who's doing the talking. Yes. No, I don't I really don't, I don't I don't have that much recollection of that, Fiona. Because I was so, you know, I was so young. Um, my mother's uh uh uh, mother and father. They lived for many, many years on, um, on Bellevue Street. And, um, my in a house on a 2 or 3 family house on on on Bellevue Street. My, um, my my father's mother, uh, and, uh, and father, uh, they they had a single, actually, a single family home on, on Worcester Street right next to, uh, Faith Congregational Church, and I all I have is, is, is fond memories of of going to generally visit, uh, most of the time, uh, my, my grandmother, my mother's side, uh, to her house on Saturdays and, and visiting with my, uh, grandmother and my dad's side on Sundays usually. And just very fond family memories of both of those. So your both your your mother's family and your father's family are fairly large. How did they disperse in the area? Did everyone sort of end up in the north and south end of Hartford? Everybody ended up, uh uh uh, in mostly in, um, in North Hartford. Um, several of my dad's brothers and sisters, along with my grandmother, um. Uh, lived in in Westbrook, in Westbrook Village. Uh, one family member lived in Stowe Village, with whom we had stayed that summer of 1955. And then there were there were others that were scattered around, but, uh, but mostly, mostly and mostly in different places in, in, in, um, In North Hartford. My dad's family was much larger than my my mom's, uh, uh, family. They were, I don't know, they were, I want to say five or 5 to 6 siblings. I can't I can't remember the exact count at the moment, but, um, I know that. Oh, pardon. Family was there were, there were, there were 11 of there were 11 of them. Right. And I know. Some of them I say some of whom obviously you're younger were were born in Hartford. Right. And then do you have any sense of when there might have been, um, a turning point in the perception of, um, public housing? I know that, um, for some of the interviews I have done, um, you know, they, they sound they sound a lot like what you had to say about Westbrook Village on history makers in terms of, um, the place being brand new, um, of having like plenty of recreation and being able to sort of re-establish a community there, since there were about 500 units or 501 units, um, that were available. Um, was there a particular turning point? Um, the way that there was for Bellevue Square when, when things perhaps were not, were not looking so well for Westbrook Village? Oh, gosh, that was much later on. I, um, uh, that was much later. Fiona. Um, but I, I, um, let's see, uh, I was, uh, I was, uh, how old was I, I was I was in third grade when we moved there and, and, uh, and I and so I had to be what? 8 or 9 years old going into third grade. And, um, we lived there until, um. Uh, it would have been 1963. Uh, a year after I graduated from Weaver High. Okay. Um. And where did your parents go? Oh, they bought a home, um, in Blue Hills Avenue. Uh, on a street called Simpson Street. Right above. Um. Right above Mark Twain School. Yes. Up the hill from where? Bowles Park. Um. Was now. Was it difficult for me? Oh, I wanted to know. Um, was it a challenge for your parents to buy a home for blacks to buy a home in a predominantly Jewish area? At that time, no. It was not. No, no, no. Now, let me just go back. Uh, one step. Uh, Fiona. When we moved to Westbrook Village, the general deal was that, you know, there were four units in a building. Um, and, uh, there was one black family, uh, generally speaking, to, uh, one of those four units. The other three were white, generally speaking. And that lasted, uh, for quite some time. And that was. That was a middle income. Um, that was a middle income project. It was a moderate. Moderate. Moderate. Moderate income. Moderate income. It was a. State moderate income program. Um. Development. Um. So. They they moved there in to Simpson Street. Bought a home in Simpson Street in 1963. And in those days, it was, um, it was fairly it was fairly um, it was fairly diverse with, uh, uh, and the Blue Hills area, but there were a lot of, uh, there were a lot of Jewish and, um, there were Italian, there were Irish, uh, some Polish. It was it was very it was a very diverse community, actually. Um, and Weaver was a, a, a very diverse student population. Uh, when I was going there in the late 50s and early 60s. Can I ask a clarification on something? So you said of the four units for Westbrook, one was set aside for black families. And so all the black families lived in that one unit, like, like lived in that one building, one of the four buildings. One building. And each and each building there were like. Oh, Well, gosh. There were. Let's see, there were. I should know this off the top of my head since I'm redeveloping it now. There were three 361 units throughout Westbrook Village. Okay. Um, most of the buildings. Um, were. Uh, had had four units, one of which. Uh. Had an African American family. And it was earmarked that way specifically. Well, I think I mean, it just I assume it was, but I don't know the answer to that. I mean, that's the way it played out. Right. Um. And all of this was, I think, you know, designed to, um, be sure that there was, um, uh, there was, uh, there was integration, uh, of the of the of the project. Um, Bowles was, um, Bowles Park. Uh, was almost the same. I think they were. I could be wrong about this, but, uh, I think that was the same kind of idea there as well. Stowe village was a was a much more, um, African American, uh, than white. But those those are the those are the three projects that I really, really remember. And, um, what does it mean for you to have, um, spent your, your formative childhood years, um, there through, um, high school and now to be redeveloping it? What what how does that influence your vision? Well for me to have grown up there and had such a fond memory of growing up there. Um, and at, you know, at one point in my journey, I was, uh, you know, I was the state senator for the second senatorial district, uh, from 77 to, uh, 1980. It was, um, it was, uh, Westbrook Village was a part of, uh, as was most of North Hartford, uh, uh, part of my constituency, um, along with a part of Windsor. Um, so if you I want to say anything north of Farmington Avenue, going all the way to the Hartford Windsor line. That was my constituency. All of it, uh, up to, uh, obviously the the Hartford, uh, West Hartford Line on, um, on Bloomfield Avenue up to Prospect Avenue. So, um, if for me to have the opportunity to be part of a, um, the development team that uh, uh, had, uh, uh, is, uh. Has torn down and and recreating a new community there of mixed income housing is, uh, is, um, a legacy project that for me, it means a lot to me. When the when the project is, um, finished. What relationship do you think it's going to have to the old Westwood Village? Or when the new folks move, in what sense are they going to be able his like sense of history? Are they going to be able to have what was there before? Oh. Well, I think, um. I think I would answer that in two ways. For those who were were there when I was growing up, um, through, um. At least. The 80s. I think they would have very fond memories of of Westbrook. Through the. 80s. Okay. Yeah. But I, I say that generally. Fiona is my recollection can um, be um, I think that in the early, uh. I want to say probably around the, in the early 2000, um, era, um, the housing authority had a hard time, um, being able to keep up with, uh, the, the infrastructure and the repairs that needed to take place, and, um. While it was home for a lot of people, I think there. Um, for some people, um, it was um, it was, you know, it was it was it was rundown. It had, it had it had really served its useful life. Which is why at the end of the day, um, it, uh, had to be replaced. It, you know, it it it served an awful lot of families, um, over about a 60 year period. Um, but it was time that, uh, it was it was time that it had something different had to happen there to increase the quality of life of people who were living there. Um, some of whom have the, the first right to to come back into the, into the new housing development now called the Village at Park River, which is what it's called now. Is there a way. I can take responsibility for that name? Uh, the village at Park River, I was. That's what I was just going to ask you. What are you trying to conjure? The old Park River memories. Uh. Well. Two things. So one, that, um. While people may have some fondness for, um, the name Westbrook Village, it it it wasn't until, um, I, I we started looking up the history of why it was named Westbrook Village in the first place. Come to find out, it was because the, uh, the chairman of the board of the Housing Authority at the time, um, was, uh, uh, it was his last name. It was his family name. And so for, you know, it. It may have had some meaning, um, to, to some of us, but not knowing the history of it, I don't think many people knew the history of it, uh, even know the history of it. Um, but, uh, what really, uh, is, um, is prominent in why I wanted to change the name is that, you know, there is a there is a major river that that runs through it. And I wanted to keep the thought that, um, of a village, you know, that, uh, that old saying it takes a village to raise a child. And and for me, um, the village, uh, raised a whole lot of children, and and I say that in and and thoughtful and loving way. And so to marry those two concepts together, um, that we hope that it will continue to be, um, a village that will raise an awful lot of children and a lot of different families, um, going forward, uh, as it is being redeveloped. What are some of the Design elements that you're including to sort of cultivate that sense of, um. Community within the overall development. Well, it's, uh, the units themselves are. Are spacious. Um, they're modern day townhouses. Um, there are a lot of amenities, um, uh, including. Bike paths and walking paths and, uh, playgrounds. So, um, for families and, uh, and children. It's a welcoming environment. Um, we're going to be maintaining a lot of the greenery and, and. You know, um, tree lines and, uh. So it's a welcoming it's a welcoming environment with this many, um, amenities as you can possibly, um, put there. Uh, but no swimming pool. Can you give me a sense of, um, I know that, um, housing was a part of your, um, portfolio as state senator. Can you, um, sort of give me a sense of the your your work as a lawyer and then your transition to real estate? Um, real estate development? Um. Well, I've been very I've been very fortunate to, uh, have to have a very diverse career. Uh, Fiona and, uh, uh, broken a number of barriers. And along the way, um, uh, having gone to Howard and Howard Law School and returning home and, and, uh, became the first African American lawyer in a major law firm, uh, in the greater Hartford area, and, uh, uh, ended up spending, um, several years there and then went into public life as a state senator, um, elected a state senator. And, uh, when I was a young lawyer, I, I was, um, one of my earliest clients was a client called the New Samaritan Corporation, which was supported by the, uh, Congregational church. United Church of Christ. Um, congregation. Um, the Congregational church is pretty big in Connecticut, and in those days it still is, but it's not as big as it used to be. And, um, my first major client was the New Samaritan Corporation, which, um, had as its focus the development of senior housing, senior citizen housing around the state. So early on, uh, in the, in the 70s, I got involved in housing, and I ended up being when I got elected to the Senate, I was the chairman of the Planning and Urban Planning and Development Committee, which oversaw all of, uh, commerce and, and and housing. Uh, I, I led the legislation to create the. First. State Department of Housing so very deeply involved in in that arena. All along the way. Why did you move? Why did you want to sever commerce and housing so that housing could be its own? Yes. Yes, exactly. It needed to be separate. As far as I was concerned. Because there was such a need for a, a focus, um, on, on developing, um, housing of all kinds, uh, particularly affordable housing. Right. Do you feel like the. Oh, sorry. No. And then I ended up, uh, uh, leaving elected political life. Um, because our our children at the time, twin sons, Adam and Chris were 11 and and our daughter Robyn was five years old, and I, um. I, uh, practicing law and being in state senate was it was two full time. Sounds like four full time jobs. Two full time jobs. And I felt that I was missing there growing up. And so I did not run for a third term and, uh, and, uh, went into the private sector, uh, at the Aetna, uh, in their law department and ended up, um, overseeing, um, our corporate responsibility investment activity for the company across the country. Um, and then eventually becoming head of the Aetna Foundation and um, which was our grant making money grant making program. Opportunity and social investment opportunity. So I led an effort to rebuild a number of urban communities across the country, including, um, Park Slope and and Brooklyn. And when it was in pretty bad shape, a couple of neighborhoods in Chicago, uh, other places. And so throughout my career, I, um, had been associated with housing and, and development and some, some way, um, when I left the at, I ended up in another big area for me was um, was being involved in, um, Fighting discrimination, bias, bigotry, racism and ended up becoming, uh, president and CEO of something that was originally called the National Conference of Christians and Jews, headquartered in New York, with some 60 regional offices in 32 states around the country. And its mission was to fight bias, bigotry and racism, promote respect and understanding among all races, religions, and cultures through advocacy, conflict resolution, and education. So those two things, um, I was president of that organization, National Organization for, uh, for ten years. And then I came back home to Hartford. Um. Uh, my sons wanted to have us do something together, And so we ended up, uh, creating the cloud company, um, as a real estate development and business investment organization back in 2005. Um, but it really grew up in the 60s and 70s. Right. Uh, at one point, it may still be Hartford was actually the the second largest West Indian Jamaican community outside of New York City, outside of Brooklyn. It's the. Third now. lines.line: # Childhood Aspirations and Achievements

> The speaker reminisces about childhood experiences of picking apples from big houses near the river and how these experiences led to a personal vow to one day own such a property. This aspiration was realized in 1982 when the speaker purchased a mansion on Woodside Circle.

metadata.title: Childhood Aspirations and Achievements
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reminisces about childhood experiences of picking apples from big houses near the river and how these experiences led to a personal vow to one day own such a property. This aspiration was realized in 1982 when the speaker purchased a mansion on Woodside Circle.

lines.line: # The Park River and Historical Flood

> The speaker discusses the Park River, known informally as the Hog River, and its historical course through Hartford. They recount the major flood of 1955, which led to the evacuation of their apartment and spending part of the summer at an aunt's apartment in Stowe Village.

metadata.title: The Park River and Historical Flood
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the Park River, known informally as the Hog River, and its historical course through Hartford. They recount the major flood of 1955, which led to the evacuation of their apartment and spending part of the summer at an aunt's apartment in Stowe Village.

lines.line: # Housing Developments and Community Impact

> The speaker reflects on the significance of new housing developments like Westbrook Village, Bowles Park, and Stowe Village in Hartford during the early 1950s. These developments represented a turning point for housing access and availability, attracting many families from different parts of North Hartford.

metadata.title: Housing Developments and Community Impact
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the significance of new housing developments like Westbrook Village, Bowles Park, and Stowe Village in Hartford during the early 1950s. These developments represented a turning point for housing access and availability, attracting many families from different parts of North Hartford.

lines.line: # Family History and Community Diversity

> The speaker shares fond family memories and discusses how their large family dispersed within North Hartford. They highlight the diversity of the community, including the Jewish, Italian, Irish, and Polish populations, and the diversity of the student population at Weaver High School.

metadata.title: Family History and Community Diversity
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares fond family memories and discusses how their large family dispersed within North Hartford. They highlight the diversity of the community, including the Jewish, Italian, Irish, and Polish populations, and the diversity of the student population at Weaver High School.

lines.line: # Integration Policy at Westbrook Village

> The speaker explains the integration policy at Westbrook Village, where typically one black family would live in one of the four units in a building. This policy was part of an effort to ensure integration within the housing project.

metadata.title: Integration Policy at Westbrook Village
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains the integration policy at Westbrook Village, where typically one black family would live in one of the four units in a building. This policy was part of an effort to ensure integration within the housing project.

lines.line: # Career Path and Housing Involvement

> The speaker outlines their diverse career, including breaking barriers as the first African American lawyer in a major Hartford law firm, serving as a state senator, and leading housing development initiatives. They also discuss their work in fighting discrimination and their return to Hartford to start a real estate development company with their sons.

metadata.title: Career Path and Housing Involvement
metadata.synopsis: The speaker outlines their diverse career, including breaking barriers as the first African American lawyer in a major Hartford law firm, serving as a state senator, and leading housing development initiatives. They also discuss their work in fighting discrimination and their return to Hartford to start a real estate development company with their sons.

lines.line: # Housing Reform and Vision for the Future

> The speaker discusses the importance of housing reform and their vision for the future, including the development of mixed-income housing and the legacy of their current project, the Village at Park River. They emphasize the need for amenities and a welcoming environment to foster community.

metadata.title: Housing Reform and Vision for the Future
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the importance of housing reform and their vision for the future, including the development of mixed-income housing and the legacy of their current project, the Village at Park River. They emphasize the need for amenities and a welcoming environment to foster community.

lines.line: # Personal Connection to Hartford

> The speaker expresses a strong personal connection to Hartford, citing it as a great place to grow up and raise a family. Despite working in New York City for a period, Hartford remained their home, and they see potential and character in the area that keeps them rooted there.

metadata.title: Personal Connection to Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The speaker expresses a strong personal connection to Hartford, citing it as a great place to grow up and raise a family. Despite working in New York City for a period, Hartford remained their home, and they see potential and character in the area that keeps them rooted there.

lines.line: # Childhood Memories and Aspirations

> The speaker recalls fond childhood experiences of picking apples from the properties of large houses near the river. These memories are intertwined with a youthful ambition to one day own one of these grand homes. This dream is eventually realized when the speaker purchases a mansion on Woodside Circle in 1982, fulfilling a long-held aspiration rooted in early experiences.

metadata.title: Childhood Memories and Aspirations
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recalls fond childhood experiences of picking apples from the properties of large houses near the river. These memories are intertwined with a youthful ambition to one day own one of these grand homes. This dream is eventually realized when the speaker purchases a mansion on Woodside Circle in 1982, fulfilling a long-held aspiration rooted in early experiences.

lines.line: # The Park (Hog) River and the 1955 Flood

> The speaker discusses the history of the Park River, also known as the Hog River, and its path through Hartford. The narrative shifts to the significant 1955 flood, which caused the river to rise and forced the speaker's family, along with others on Mark Twain Drive, to evacuate their apartment. The family spent much of that summer living with an aunt in Stow Village, highlighting the impact of natural disasters on local residents.

metadata.title: The Park (Hog) River and the 1955 Flood
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the history of the Park River, also known as the Hog River, and its path through Hartford. The narrative shifts to the significant 1955 flood, which caused the river to rise and forced the speaker's family, along with others on Mark Twain Drive, to evacuate their apartment. The family spent much of that summer living with an aunt in Stow Village, highlighting the impact of natural disasters on local residents.

lines.line: # Public Housing as a Turning Point in Hartford

> The conversation explores the significance of new public housing developments like Westbrook Village, Bowles Park, and Stow Village in Hartford during the early 1950s. The speaker reflects on how these projects represented a major shift in housing access and availability, especially as many families moved from older neighborhoods into these modern developments. The sense of renewal and opportunity associated with these new homes is emphasized as a pivotal moment for the community.

metadata.title: Public Housing as a Turning Point in Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The conversation explores the significance of new public housing developments like Westbrook Village, Bowles Park, and Stow Village in Hartford during the early 1950s. The speaker reflects on how these projects represented a major shift in housing access and availability, especially as many families moved from older neighborhoods into these modern developments. The sense of renewal and opportunity associated with these new homes is emphasized as a pivotal moment for the community.

lines.line: # Family Migration and Neighborhood Memories

> The interviewer asks about the family's experiences with the Bellevue Square project and the changes it brought to the neighborhood. The speaker admits to having limited recollection due to their young age but shares memories of grandparents and extended family living on Bellevue and Worcester Streets. These recollections are filled with nostalgia for family visits and the close-knit community that existed before the move to public housing.

metadata.title: Family Migration and Neighborhood Memories
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks about the family's experiences with the Bellevue Square project and the changes it brought to the neighborhood. The speaker admits to having limited recollection due to their young age but shares memories of grandparents and extended family living on Bellevue and Worcester Streets. These recollections are filled with nostalgia for family visits and the close-knit community that existed before the move to public housing.

lines.line: # Family Dispersal and Settlement in North Hartford

> The discussion turns to the size and dispersal of the speaker's extended family. Most relatives settled in North Hartford, with several living in Westbrook Village and Stow Village, while others were scattered throughout the area. The speaker notes that their father's family was particularly large, with up to eleven siblings, and describes the migration patterns that shaped their family's presence in Hartford.

metadata.title: Family Dispersal and Settlement in North Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to the size and dispersal of the speaker's extended family. Most relatives settled in North Hartford, with several living in Westbrook Village and Stow Village, while others were scattered throughout the area. The speaker notes that their father's family was particularly large, with up to eleven siblings, and describes the migration patterns that shaped their family's presence in Hartford.

lines.line: # Integration and Homeownership in Blue Hills Avenue

> The interviewer inquires about the challenges of buying a home as a Black family in the predominantly Jewish Blue Hills Avenue area during the 1960s. The speaker explains that it was not difficult and describes the diversity of the neighborhood, which included Jewish, Italian, Irish, and Polish residents. The speaker also recounts the process of moving from Westbrook Village to Simpson Street and highlights the moderate-income nature of the public housing project.

metadata.title: Integration and Homeownership in Blue Hills Avenue
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer inquires about the challenges of buying a home as a Black family in the predominantly Jewish Blue Hills Avenue area during the 1960s. The speaker explains that it was not difficult and describes the diversity of the neighborhood, which included Jewish, Italian, Irish, and Polish residents. The speaker also recounts the process of moving from Westbrook Village to Simpson Street and highlights the moderate-income nature of the public housing project.

lines.line: # Racial Integration Policies in Public Housing

> The interviewer seeks clarification on the racial integration policies in Westbrook Village, leading the speaker to explain that typically one of the four units in each building was occupied by a Black family, while the others were white. This arrangement was likely intended to ensure integration within the project. The speaker notes that Bowles Park had a similar setup, while Stow Village was predominantly African American, reflecting different approaches to integration across Hartford's public housing developments.

metadata.title: Racial Integration Policies in Public Housing
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer seeks clarification on the racial integration policies in Westbrook Village, leading the speaker to explain that typically one of the four units in each building was occupied by a Black family, while the others were white. This arrangement was likely intended to ensure integration within the project. The speaker notes that Bowles Park had a similar setup, while Stow Village was predominantly African American, reflecting different approaches to integration across Hartford's public housing developments.

lines.line: # Personal Connection and Redevelopment Vision

> The speaker reflects on the deep personal significance of having grown up in Westbrook Village and later representing the area as a state senator. Now involved in redeveloping the site, the speaker views the project as a legacy, aiming to create a new mixed-income community that honors the area's history. The opportunity to shape the future of a place so central to their own life is described as profoundly meaningful.

metadata.title: Personal Connection and Redevelopment Vision
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the deep personal significance of having grown up in Westbrook Village and later representing the area as a state senator. Now involved in redeveloping the site, the speaker views the project as a legacy, aiming to create a new mixed-income community that honors the area's history. The opportunity to shape the future of a place so central to their own life is described as profoundly meaningful.

lines.line: # Decline and Transformation: From Westbrook Village to the Village at Park River

> The conversation addresses the decline of Westbrook Village in the early 2000s, when maintenance and infrastructure issues led to deteriorating conditions. The speaker explains the decision to redevelop the area, giving former residents the first right to return. The new development is named the Village at Park River, a choice inspired by both the river's presence and the desire to evoke the supportive, communal spirit of a 'village' that raised many children.

metadata.title: Decline and Transformation: From Westbrook Village to the Village at Park River
metadata.synopsis: The conversation addresses the decline of Westbrook Village in the early 2000s, when maintenance and infrastructure issues led to deteriorating conditions. The speaker explains the decision to redevelop the area, giving former residents the first right to return. The new development is named the Village at Park River, a choice inspired by both the river's presence and the desire to evoke the supportive, communal spirit of a 'village' that raised many children.

lines.line: # Designing for Community in the New Development

> The speaker describes the features of the new housing development, emphasizing spacious, modern townhouses and a variety of amenities such as bike paths, walking trails, and playgrounds. The design aims to maintain greenery and create a welcoming environment that fosters community among residents. The focus is on providing as many amenities as possible to support families and children, though there will be no swimming pool.

metadata.title: Designing for Community in the New Development

The design aims to maintain greenery and create a welcoming environment that fosters community among residents. The focus is on providing as many amenities as possible to support families and children, though there will be no swimming pool.

lines.line: # Career Path: Law, Politics, and Housing Development

> The speaker outlines a diverse career that began with legal practice after graduating from Howard Law School, becoming the first African American lawyer at a major Hartford firm. Early involvement with the New Samaritan Corporation introduced the speaker to senior housing development. As a state senator, the speaker chaired the Urban Planning and Development Committee, leading efforts to create Connecticut's first State Department of Housing and shaping housing policy.

metadata.title: Career Path: Law, Politics, and Housing Development
metadata.synopsis: The speaker outlines a diverse career that began with legal practice after graduating from Howard Law School, becoming the first African American lawyer at a major Hartford firm. Early involvement with the New Samaritan Corporation introduced the speaker to senior housing development. As a state senator, the speaker chaired the Urban Planning and Development Committee, leading efforts to create Connecticut's first State Department of Housing and shaping housing policy.

lines.line: # Separating Housing from Commerce and Career Transitions

> The speaker explains the rationale for separating housing from commerce in state government, emphasizing the need for focused attention on affordable housing. The narrative then covers the decision to leave elected office to spend more time with family, leading to a career at Aetna in corporate responsibility and philanthropy. The speaker describes overseeing investments in urban redevelopment projects across the country and managing the Aetna Foundation's grant-making activities.

metadata.title: Separating Housing from Commerce and Career Transitions
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains the rationale for separating housing from commerce in state government, emphasizing the need for focused attention on affordable housing. The narrative then covers the decision to leave elected office to spend more time with family, leading to a career at Aetna in corporate responsibility and philanthropy. The speaker describes overseeing investments in urban redevelopment projects across the country and managing the Aetna Foundation's grant-making activities.

lines.line: # Fighting Discrimination and Founding the Cloud Company

> After leaving politics, the speaker became president and CEO of the National Conference of Christians and Jews, leading efforts to combat bias, bigotry, and racism nationwide. Upon returning to Hartford, the speaker and his sons founded the Cloud Company, a real estate development and investment firm. This transition marks a continued commitment to community development and social justice through both advocacy and business.

metadata.title: Fighting Discrimination and Founding the Cloud Company

Upon returning to Hartford, the speaker and his sons founded the Cloud Company, a real estate development and investment firm. This transition marks a continued commitment to community development and social justice through both advocacy and business.

lines.line: # West Indian Community and Homeownership in Hartford

> Prompted by a question about West Indian homeownership, the speaker discusses the growth of Hartford's West Indian community, particularly among Jamaicans, since World War II. While the speaker was not heavily involved in individual homeownership transactions, he developed close relationships with community members through political and social activities. Hartford is noted as having one of the largest West Indian populations in the United States.

metadata.title: West Indian Community and Homeownership in Hartford

Hartford is noted as having one of the largest West Indian populations in the United States.

lines.line: # Commitment to Hartford and Reasons for Staying

> The interviewer asks about the speaker's decision to return to Hartford after studying elsewhere. The speaker affirms a strong attachment to Hartford, maintaining a home there even while working in New York. The area is described as an excellent place to raise a family, with enduring character and potential, making it the speaker's permanent home despite opportunities elsewhere.

lines.line: # Zoning Reform, Affordable Housing, and Future Prospects

> The final discussion centers on the prospects for zoning reform and affordable housing in Connecticut. The speaker expresses optimism that recent legislative changes and state financing mechanisms will encourage regionalization and expand housing opportunities. While acknowledging ongoing political challenges, the speaker believes these efforts will lead to meaningful improvements in affordable housing over time.

children.text: # Childhood Memories and Aspirations

> The speaker recalls fond childhood experiences of picking apples from the properties of large houses near the river. These memories are intertwined with a youthful ambition to one day own one of these grand homes. This dream is eventually realized when the speaker purchases a mansion on Woodside Circle in 1982, fulfilling a long-held aspiration rooted in early experiences.
# The Park (Hog) River and the 1955 Flood

> The speaker discusses the history of the Park River, also known as the Hog River, and its path through Hartford. The narrative shifts to the significant 1955 flood, which caused the river to rise and forced the speaker's family, along with others on Mark Twain Drive, to evacuate their apartment. The family spent much of that summer living with an aunt in Stow Village, highlighting the impact of natural disasters on local residents.

story.title: Sanford Cloud partical 38mins
story.description: The interview provided insights into the history and redevelopment of housing in Hartford, particularly focusing on Westbrook Village, now rebranded as the Village at Park River. The conversation explored the impact of public housing projects developed in the 1950s and their significance in offering new housing opportunities, addressing integration policies, and the subsequent decline and redevelopment efforts. The interviewee reflected on the nostalgic and personal significance of these developments, having grown up in the area and later participating in its redevelopment. Broader discussions included the evolution of housing access policies, the need for zoning reform to address affordable housing, and the interviewee's diverse career spanning law, politics, and real estate development, including efforts to combat discrimination and promote social investment.
